title: Layer norm
description: Per-token mean-and-variance normalization that rescales each hidden vector before the next sublayer in a transformer block.
openingSummary: Layer norm keeps each token's hidden vector in a predictable range by using that token's own feature mean and variance, which makes it a clean fit for transformer blocks that cannot rely on stable minibatch statistics.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Layer normalization rescales one token's hidden vector by subtracting its feature mean, dividing by a stabilized feature standard deviation, then applying learned scale and shift. The statistics come from that one vector, not from the rest of the batch.

whyItExists.title: Why It Exists
whyItExists.body: Layer norm targets training stability in deep sequence models where batch statistics are either noisy or structurally inconvenient. It keeps residual-stream magnitudes from drifting too far before attention or feed-forward sublayers run.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: For one token position, layer norm measures the mean and variance across the hidden features in that vector, recenters and rescales the vector, then applies learned gain and bias. The switcher below keeps that path next to RMSNorm so you can see that the extra mean-centering step is the main structural difference.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: These two formulas apply to one token vector at a time. Layer norm subtracts the feature mean before scaling, while RMSNorm keeps only the scale correction.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Layer norm stays within one token, unlike batch norm, and it includes mean centering, unlike RMSNorm. Group norm also avoids batch coupling, but it normalizes channel groups rather than an entire token vector.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Layer norm appears throughout the original transformer family and many later encoder-decoder or decoder stacks. Even when a newer model switches to RMSNorm, layer norm remains the reference point for comparison.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: Layer norm adds mean-centering work that RMSNorm skips, and that extra step is not always needed. Some modern decoder stacks prefer the lighter RMSNorm rule when they want similar stability with slightly less computation.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: Layer norm is still one of the clearest ways to understand how transformer normalization works. Many papers explain newer normalization tricks by contrasting them with the layer norm baseline.

computeFlow.alt: Normalization comparison showing LayerNorm and RMSNorm as two per-token normalization paths in the same transformer slot

layer.meanCentering: Yes; subtracts the token's feature mean
layer.statisticsScope: One token or hidden vector at a time

rms.meanCentering: No; scales by root mean square only
rms.statisticsScope: One token or hidden vector at a time
rms.transformerFit: Very common in newer decoder-only LLMs

batch.meanCentering: Yes; uses the minibatch feature mean
batch.statisticsScope: One feature or channel across the minibatch
batch.transformerFit: Usually awkward for autoregressive token streams

x.definition: Hidden feature vector at one token position.

mu.definition: Mean of the features in x.

sigma2.definition: Variance of the features in x.

x.definition: Hidden feature vector at one token position.

tokenStats.label: Feature mean μ and variance σ²

normalizeToken.label: Subtract mean and divide by standard deviation

learnedAffine.label: Apply learned gain γ and bias β

perTokenStats.label: Statistics come from this one token vector, not from the batch

centeringBenefit.label: Mean centering makes the output zero-centered before the learned affine step
